puzzles.headline: The Fall of the Berlin Wall
puzzles.description: After 28 years dividing East and West Berlin, the concrete barrier came down in a single chaotic night. Thousands of citizens surged to the checkpoints after a confused government spokesman seemed to announce the border was open. Within hours, people were dancing on the wall and chipping away at it with hammers.

monday: This happened during the final chapter of a decades-long geopolitical standoff.
The leader of the Soviet Union had recently introduced sweeping reforms.
The city had been divided since a wall was built in 1961.
tuesday: This occurred in a year of significant upheaval across Eastern Europe.
A confused bureaucrat accidentally triggered the events.
The wall had divided the same city for 28 years.
wednesday: This was a major geopolitical event in the latter half of the 20th century.
The year saw significant changes in European governance.
Mikhail Gorbachev's reforms helped make this possible.
thursday: This occurred in the second half of the 20th century.
A Cold War milestone fell in this year.
friday: This happened during the Cold War era.
The event took place in a divided European capital.
saturday: The world was watching something else unfold that day.

puzzles.context_card: For 28 years, Checkpoint Charlie was the defining symbol of the Cold War. On November 9th, 1989, a confused bureaucrat announced that East Germans could cross freely — and thousands flooded through. No one had planned the announcement. The wall fell because the system was already crumbling.

puzzles.event_formal: Release of Nelson Mandela from Victor Verster Prison
puzzles.headline: After 27 years behind bars, the world held its breath
puzzles.description: He walked out of Victor Verster Prison outside Cape Town, raised his fist, and the crowd erupted. His release marked the beginning of the end of apartheid in South Africa — and one of the most watched moments in television history.

monday: This occurred near the end of the Cold War era.
It marked a turning point in a nation divided by racial segregation.
The man released would go on to lead his country within a few years.
tuesday: This moment ended a nearly three-decade imprisonment.
The event took place in southern Africa.
The man freed had been convicted of sabotage and conspiracy.
wednesday: This was a watershed moment in the struggle against apartheid.
The prisoner had been held since the early 1960s.
His release was broadcast live around the world.
thursday: A political prisoner walked free after 27 years.
This happened in post-Cold War Africa.
friday: A man imprisoned for over two decades emerged to lead a nation.
This was one of the most-watched moments of its decade.
saturday: The long walk ended at a prison gate outside Cape Town.

puzzles.context_card: Nelson Mandela had spent 18 of his 27 years imprisoned on Robben Island, a bleak outcrop in Table Bay. By the time he walked free on February 11th, 1990, he had become the most famous political prisoner in the world. Within four years, he was president.

puzzles.event_formal: Assassination of President John F. Kennedy
puzzles.headline: Shots rang out in Dealey Plaza, and everything changed
puzzles.description: President Kennedy was riding in a motorcade through downtown Dallas when shots were fired from the sixth floor of a nearby building. He was pronounced dead at Parkland Memorial Hospital thirty minutes later. Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson was sworn in aboard Air Force One, Jackie Kennedy standing beside him.

monday: This occurred during the Cold War, in a decade defined by tension and change.
A president was killed in a southern American city.
The assassin was himself killed two days later before he could stand trial.
tuesday: This was a defining moment in American political history.
The event took place during a presidential motorcade.
The Warren Commission investigated what happened.
wednesday: A US president was killed during a public appearance.
This happened in the early 1960s.
The event shocked a nation still living through the space race.
thursday: A president was assassinated during a motorcade.
This occurred in early Cold War America.
friday: Dallas, Texas was the site of a pivotal American tragedy.
The event happened before the mid-1960s.
saturday: A motorcade, a plaza, and a moment the world could not unsee.

puzzles.context_card: Lee Harvey Oswald fired three shots from the sixth floor of the Texas School Book Depository on November 22nd, 1963. Kennedy was pronounced dead at 1:00 PM. Two days later, Oswald was shot by nightclub owner Jack Ruby in the Dallas Police Department basement — live on national television. The truth of what happened that day has never fully left public debate.

puzzles.event: D-Day: Allied Forces Land at Normandy
puzzles.event_formal: Operation Overlord — D-Day Landings at Normandy
puzzles.headline: The largest seaborne invasion in history began before dawn
puzzles.description: Over 156,000 Allied troops crossed the English Channel and stormed five beaches along a 50-mile stretch of heavily fortified French coastline. The operation, code-named Overlord, marked the beginning of the liberation of Western Europe from Nazi occupation.

monday: This was a pivotal moment in a global conflict that defined the mid-20th century.
Allied forces from multiple nations participated.
The operation involved a massive naval and airborne component.
tuesday: This was the largest amphibious military operation ever attempted.
It took place on the northern coast of France.
The date was kept secret from Nazi forces using an elaborate deception campaign.
wednesday: Allied forces stormed fortified beaches in a surprise assault.
This happened during the Second World War.
Eisenhower commanded the overall operation.
thursday: Thousands of Allied troops landed on French beaches at dawn.
This was a turning point in World War II.
friday: Code-named Overlord, this was the largest seaborne invasion in history.
It opened the Western Front against Nazi Germany.
saturday: Five beaches. One dawn. The fate of Europe.

puzzles.context_card: On June 6th, 1944, the Allies launched the most complex military operation in history. Paratroopers had already dropped behind enemy lines by the time the first landing craft hit the beaches at 6:30 AM. The cost was enormous — over 4,000 Allied soldiers died on D-Day alone — but within a year, Germany had surrendered.

puzzles.event: The Atomic Bomb Drops on Hiroshima
puzzles.event_formal: United States atomic bombing of Hiroshima, Japan
puzzles.headline: A single bomb flattened a city and changed warfare forever
puzzles.description: At 8:15 in the morning, a B-29 bomber called the Enola Gay released a uranium bomb nicknamed Little Boy over the Japanese city of Hiroshima. The explosion instantly killed tens of thousands and flattened nearly five square miles of the city.

monday: This occurred near the end of the Second World War.
A new and unprecedented weapon was used for the first time in warfare.
The target was a Japanese city chosen partly because it had not been heavily bombed before.
tuesday: This was a decisive moment in the Pacific theater of World War II.
The weapon used had been developed in secret over several years.
It led directly to a major power's unconditional surrender.
wednesday: An American aircraft dropped a new type of weapon on a major city.
This occurred in the 1940s, during a global war.
The event introduced the atomic age.
thursday: A single bomb destroyed a Japanese city in seconds.
This occurred in the final months of World War II.
friday: Little Boy fell from the Enola Gay at 8:15 AM.
This moment ended one era and began another.
saturday: A flash of light over a city, and the world was never the same.

puzzles.context_card: The Manhattan Project had cost two billion dollars and employed hundreds of thousands of people — most of whom had no idea what they were building. On August 6th, 1945, the result was dropped on Hiroshima. Three days later, a second bomb fell on Nagasaki. Japan surrendered on August 15th. The atomic age had begun.

puzzles.event: Lindbergh Completes First Solo Transatlantic Flight
puzzles.event_formal: Charles Lindbergh's solo nonstop transatlantic flight, New York to Paris
puzzles.headline: A 25-year-old flew alone across the Atlantic for 33 hours
puzzles.description: Charles Lindbergh took off from Roosevelt Field in New York and landed at Le Bourget Airfield outside Paris, completing the first solo nonstop transatlantic flight. He had no radio, limited instruments, and five sandwiches. A crowd of 100,000 awaited his landing.

monday: This occurred in the interwar period, between the two World Wars.
The achievement involved crossing the Atlantic Ocean.
A young American aviator became an overnight global celebrity.
tuesday: This set a record that no one had broken before.
The pilot flew alone for more than 30 hours.
The journey ended in France.
wednesday: An American pilot completed a solo flight from New York to Paris.
This happened in the 1920s.
The feat won a ,000 prize.
thursday: A solo pilot crossed the Atlantic nonstop.
This happened before the Great Depression.
friday: The Spirit of St. Louis touched down outside Paris.
This was the first solo nonstop transatlantic flight.
saturday: Thirty-three hours, five sandwiches, and the sky between two continents.

puzzles.context_card: The Spirit of St. Louis was so loaded with fuel at takeoff that it barely cleared the telephone wires at the end of the runway. Charles Lindbergh landed at Le Bourget at 10:22 PM on May 21st, 1927, and was immediately mobbed by the crowd. He became the most famous man in the world overnight — and remained so for the rest of his life, for better and worse.

puzzles.event_formal: Kennedy's address revealing Soviet missiles in Cuba
puzzles.headline: The president went on television and told the world how close it was
puzzles.description: President Kennedy addressed the nation to reveal that American spy planes had photographed Soviet nuclear missile sites under construction in Cuba. He announced a naval blockade of the island and demanded the Soviets remove the missiles — bringing the world to the edge of nuclear war.
puzzles.context_card: For thirteen days in October 1962, the world came closer to nuclear war than it ever has before or since. Soviet ships carrying additional missiles turned back at the US naval blockade. Soviet leader Khrushchev eventually agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a US pledge not to invade Cuba and a secret agreement to remove American missiles from Turkey. The crisis was resolved — but only barely.

puzzles.event: Rosa Parks Refuses to Give Up Her Seat
puzzles.event_formal: Rosa Parks' arrest on the Montgomery, Alabama city bus
puzzles.headline: One woman stayed seated, and a movement stood up
puzzles.description: Rosa Parks, a seamstress and NAACP secretary, refused to give up her bus seat to a white passenger in Montgomery, Alabama. She was arrested and charged with violating segregation laws. Her arrest sparked the Montgomery Bus Boycott, a 381-day protest that became a defining moment of the Civil Rights Movement.

monday: This occurred during the early years of the Civil Rights Movement.
The act of defiance took place on public transportation in the American South.
The aftermath lasted over a year and led to a Supreme Court ruling.
tuesday: A woman refused to comply with a segregation law.
This happened in Alabama in the mid-20th century.
Her arrest triggered a year-long boycott.
wednesday: This act of civil disobedience occurred in Montgomery, Alabama.
It happened in the 1950s.
Martin Luther King Jr. led the protest that followed.
thursday: A woman's refusal to move on a bus sparked a national movement.
This occurred in the mid-1950s American South.
friday: Rosa Parks was arrested for not giving up her seat.
The event ignited the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
saturday: She stayed seated. The movement stood up.

puzzles.context_card: Rosa Parks was not simply tired from work when she refused to move — she was tired of giving in. She had been trained at the Highlander Folk School, a center for civil rights activism. Her arrest on December 1st, 1955 was strategic: the NAACP had been looking for a test case. The resulting boycott lasted 381 days and ended with the Supreme Court ruling bus segregation unconstitutional.

puzzles.event_formal: Space Shuttle Challenger disintegration on launch
puzzles.headline: Millions watching live saw it break apart 73 seconds after liftoff
puzzles.description: The Space Shuttle Challenger broke apart 73 seconds into its flight, killing all seven crew members including Christa McAuliffe, who would have been the first teacher in space. The disaster was caused by O-ring failure in the unusually cold temperatures at launch. President Reagan postponed his State of the Union address.

monday: This occurred during the Space Shuttle program era.
A catastrophic failure happened shortly after launch.
The crew included a civilian teacher selected through a public contest.
tuesday: This was one of the most-watched disasters in American history.
A spacecraft failed 73 seconds into its mission.
The event led to a major suspension of the shuttle program.
wednesday: A NASA launch ended in tragedy on a cold January morning.
This happened in the 1980s.
The crew of seven included the first teacher chosen for spaceflight.
thursday: The Space Shuttle broke apart seconds after launch.
This occurred in the mid-1980s.
friday: Challenger disintegrated 73 seconds into its flight.
The disaster was caused by cold temperatures affecting a rubber seal.
saturday: It was cold that morning. No one had listened to the engineers who said wait.

puzzles.context_card: Engineers at Morton Thiokol had warned NASA the night before launch that O-rings could fail in temperatures below 53°F — and it was 28°F at Cape Canaveral that morning. Their objections were overruled. The Rogers Commission that investigated the disaster famously included physicist Richard Feynman, who demonstrated the O-ring failure with a glass of ice water at a press conference.

puzzles.event_formal: First powered airplane flight by the Wright Brothers
puzzles.headline: The Wright Brothers' First Flight
puzzles.description: Two bicycle mechanics from Dayton, Ohio traveled to the windswept sand dunes of Kitty Hawk, North Carolina to test their latest flying machine. On a cold December morning, with only five witnesses present, Orville Wright lay prone on the lower wing, opened the throttle, and lifted off the ground for 12 seconds.

monday: This happened in the early years of a new century.
The inventors were two brothers who ran a bicycle shop.
The event took place on a beach known for its constant winds.
tuesday: The flight lasted less than a minute.
They had been studying bird flight to solve the problem.
The event happened in North Carolina.
wednesday: This occurred in the first decade of the 20th century.
The event happened in North America.
Only five people witnessed it.
thursday: This was an American invention that changed the world.
The first flight lasted under 60 seconds.
friday: This happened in the early 1900s.
Two brothers achieved what many thought impossible.
saturday: The inventors were brothers from Ohio.

puzzles.context_card: The Wright Brothers made four flights at Kitty Hawk on December 17th, 1903. The first lasted just 12 seconds and covered 120 feet. By the end of that day, they had flown a total of 59 seconds. Orville would later say that the sensation of flight never fully wore off on him — it just kept getting better.

puzzles.event_formal: First human landing on the Moon
puzzles.headline: The Apollo 11 Moon Landing
puzzles.description: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin descended in the lunar module Eagle while Michael Collins orbited above. Alarms blared and Armstrong had to manually guide the craft past a boulder field with only 30 seconds of fuel remaining. When the module touched down, Mission Control erupted. Six hundred million people watched the grainy footage on television.

monday: This happened in the year humans first walked on another world.
The mission was part of the space race between two superpowers.
The event fulfilled a president's promise earlier in the decade.
tuesday: The event happened in the summer and was watched by millions worldwide.
The commander was from Ohio.
The year also saw Woodstock and a famous music festival.
wednesday: This was a major achievement of the 1960s.
The mission launched from Cape Canaveral, Florida.
The famous first words were about 'one small step.'
thursday: This was in the 1960s.
The first words spoken on the surface were about this being one small step.
friday: This happened during the Space Race.
The mission was called Apollo.
saturday: This changed how we see our place in the universe.

puzzles.context_card: Neil Armstrong's first steps on the Moon were watched by an estimated 600 million people — one-fifth of the world's population. The lunar module was called Eagle. Armstrong had only 30 seconds of fuel remaining when he found a landing spot. He called it 'magnificent desolation.'

puzzles.event_formal: RMS Titanic sinks on maiden voyage
puzzles.headline: The Sinking of the Titanic
puzzles.description: The most luxurious ocean liner ever built was four days into its maiden voyage across the North Atlantic, carrying some of the wealthiest people in the world alongside hundreds of immigrants seeking new lives in America. A lookout spotted an iceberg dead ahead. Despite a hard turn, the ship's starboard side scraped along the ice, opening a fatal gash below the waterline. Over 1,500 people died.

monday: This was a maritime disaster that shocked the world.
The ship was called 'unsinkable' before its maiden voyage.
Fewer lifeboats were aboard than needed for all passengers.
tuesday: The ship was bound for New York when it struck an iceberg.
A famous film about it was released in 1997.
The ship sank in under three hours.
wednesday: This was an Edwardian-era tragedy.
It led to major changes in maritime safety regulations.
The ship carried both millionaires and immigrants.
thursday: The ship was the largest in the world at the time.
This was in the early 20th century.
friday: The ship struck an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
It was called 'unsinkable.'
saturday: It sank on its maiden voyage.

puzzles.context_card: The Titanic was carrying some of the world's wealthiest people and hundreds of immigrants seeking a new life in America. It struck the iceberg at 11:40 PM and sank in under three hours. The nearby RMS Carpathia rescued 705 survivors. The disaster led to major changes in maritime safety regulations.

puzzles.headline: The Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
puzzles.description: A young Bosnian Serb nationalist fired two shots at a royal motorcade in Sarajevo, killing Archduke Franz Ferdinand and his wife Sophie. Within weeks, a web of alliances and ultimatums dragged the great powers of Europe into World War I — a conflict that would last four years, destroy four empires, and kill 17 million people.

monday: A political assassination in the Balkans sparked this conflict.
The war involved most of the great powers of the era.
The conflict lasted four years and destroyed four empires.
tuesday: The war introduced trench warfare and new weapons of destruction.
It was triggered by an event in Sarajevo.
The assassinated leader was heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne.
wednesday: This began in the decade before the Great Depression.
The conflict involved four empires and lasted four years.
It was supposed to be 'the war to end all wars.'
thursday: A Balkan assassination triggered this war.
The conflict ended four empires.
friday: This happened in the early 20th century.
The assassination was in Sarajevo.
saturday: The assassination was in Sarajevo.

puzzles.context_card: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, set off a chain reaction of alliance obligations. What was supposed to be a short war became a four-year conflict that killed 17 million people and destroyed four empires: the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian empires.

puzzles.headline: The September 11 Attacks
puzzles.description: On a clear Tuesday morning, al-Qaeda terrorists hijacked four commercial airplanes and carried out coordinated attacks on the United States. Two planes struck the Twin Towers of the World Trade Center in New York. A third hit the Pentagon. A fourth crashed in a Pennsylvania field after passengers fought back. Nearly 3,000 people were killed.

monday: This happened in the early 21st century.
Terrorists hijacked four commercial airplanes.
It led to the creation of the Department of Homeland Security.
tuesday: Two planes struck the tallest buildings in New York City.
This led to the 'War on Terror.'
The attacks were by al-Qaeda terrorists.
wednesday: This happened on a Tuesday morning in September.
Nearly 3,000 people were killed.
It fundamentally changed American foreign policy.
thursday: This happened in the 21st century.
Two planes hit the World Trade Center.
friday: Terrorists hijacked four planes.
It changed airport security forever.
saturday: Nearly 3,000 people died that day.

puzzles.context_card: On September 11, 2001, nearly 3,000 people were killed in a series of coordinated terrorist attacks. The FBI quickly opened an investigation called PENTTBOM. Within days, President George W. Bush had signed the USA PATRIOT Act. The attacks reshaped American foreign policy, airport security, and domestic surveillance in ways still felt today.

puzzles.headline: The Beatles Arrive in America
puzzles.description: The four-piece rock band from Liverpool landed at JFK Airport in New York to absolute pandemonium. Thousands of screaming teenagers packed the terminal. Within days, they appeared on the Ed Sullivan Show, watched by 73 million viewers — the largest TV audience in American history at that point. The British Invasion of pop music had begun.

monday: A British Invasion began with this event.
The band appeared on a major American TV show shortly after arriving.
73 million viewers watched their TV appearance.
tuesday: They landed at a New York airport to screaming fans.
This occurred at the height of youth cultural revolution.
The same year, a major civil rights act was signed.
wednesday: This happened the same year a landmark civil rights law passed.
The Fab Four's arrival triggered mass hysteria.
The band was from Liverpool.
thursday: This was in the 1960s.
This year saw a British musical invasion.
friday: The band arrived from England.
It changed American pop music overnight.
saturday: The band was from Liverpool.

puzzles.context_card: When The Beatles landed at JFK Airport on February 7, 1964, they were met by 3,000 screaming fans — the largest welcome in airport history to that point. They appeared on Ed Sullivan Show ten days later, watched by 73 million viewers. Within weeks, Beatlemania had completely reshaped American pop music and youth culture.

puzzles.headline: Apple Unveils the iPhone
puzzles.description: Steve Jobs walked onto a stage in San Francisco wearing his signature black turtleneck and announced three new products: a widescreen iPod, a mobile phone, and an internet communicator. The crowd cheered each one. Then he revealed they were all the same device. No physical keyboard, no stylus — just a multi-touch glass screen that responded to your fingertips.

monday: This was unveiled by the CEO of a computer company in a keynote.
The announcement promised to revolutionize mobile phones.
The device had no physical keyboard.
tuesday: The year also saw the start of a global financial crisis.
The CEO wore his signature black turtleneck on stage.
The device used a multi-touch glass screen.
wednesday: This was in the first decade of the 21st century.
The company was founded in a garage in California.
It was unveiled in San Francisco.
thursday: This was a product announcement that changed an industry.
The device was a smartphone.
friday: It was unveiled by Steve Jobs.
The device had no physical keyboard.
saturday: It was unveiled by Steve Jobs.

puzzles.context_card: Steve Jobs unveiled the iPhone on January 9, 2007, calling it 'a revolutionary product.' It didn't go on sale until June, but it immediately changed what people expected from mobile devices. The initial model had no App Store — that came in 2008. By 2011, the iPhone had made Apple the most valuable company in the world.

puzzles.headline: The Dissolution of the Soviet Union
puzzles.description: On a cold December evening, the red hammer-and-sickle flag was lowered from the Kremlin for the last time and replaced by the Russian tricolor. The USSR — a superpower that had shaped world politics for nearly seven decades — ceased to exist, fracturing into fifteen independent nations. Mikhail Gorbachev resigned and handed the nuclear codes to Boris Yeltsin.

monday: This happened during a period of rapid political change.
The superpower was led by Mikhail Gorbachev before this happened.
The country fractured into 15 independent nations.
tuesday: The event happened around Christmas time.
The country had been a superpower since after World War II.
Reforms called 'glasnost' and 'perestroika' preceded this.
wednesday: This occurred at the end of the Cold War.
The USSR was dissolved into 15 independent states.
The hammer-and-sickle flag was lowered for the last time.
thursday: This happened in the early 1990s.
Mikhail Gorbachev resigned as the leader.
friday: The Cold War ended with this event.
It happened around Christmas.
saturday: The Cold War ended with this event.

puzzles.context_card: On December 26, 1991, the Supreme Soviet of the USSR ratified the Belovezh Accords, formally ending the Soviet Union. What had been the world's largest country by area splintered into 15 independent nations. For 69 years, the Soviet experiment had shaped global politics. Its end brought both hope for a new world order and anxiety about nuclear weapons in unstable new states.

puzzles.headline: The First Email Is Sent
puzzles.description: Ray Tomlinson, a programmer at BBN Technologies in Cambridge, Massachusetts, devised a way to send messages between users on different machines connected by ARPANET. He chose the @ symbol to separate the user name from the machine name. The first test message was so unremarkable he couldn't remember what it said — probably just 'QWERTYUIOP.'

monday: This happened at a US defense research institution.
The network would eventually evolve into the internet.
The sender chose the @ symbol for the address format.
tuesday: The first message was sent between two computers in different locations.
This predates the personal computer revolution.
The message content was something like 'QWERTYUIOP.'
wednesday: This was in the early 1970s.
The sender worked on ARPANET, a precursor to the internet.
Ray Tomlinson was the programmer.
thursday: This was at a defense research institution.
It was in the early 1970s.
friday: The network became the internet.
Ray Tomlinson sent it.
saturday: The @ symbol was born from this.

puzzles.context_card: Ray Tomlinson sent the first email in 1971, choosing the @ symbol to separate the user name from the computer name. The message itself was largely forgettable — something like 'QWERTYUIOP.' But the @ sign became Tomlinson's lasting contribution to digital communication, now used in billions of email addresses worldwide.

puzzles.event: The Great Wall of China Completed
puzzles.event_formal: Completion of the Great Wall of China
puzzles.headline: The Great Wall of China Completed
puzzles.description: Over thousands of miles of mountains, deserts, and plains, generations of workers — soldiers, peasants, and prisoners — built and rebuilt a massive fortification to protect China from northern invaders. The final major phase of construction under the Ming Dynasty ended as the dynasty fell and the Qing rose to power, completing the 13,000-mile network of walls, watchtowers, and garrison stations.

monday: This is one of the most recognizable structures in human history.
It was built to protect against invasions from the north.
Construction took place over many dynasties.
tuesday: The wall is often said to be visible from space — a popular myth.
The structure spans over 13,000 miles.
The final phase was completed as a dynasty fell.
wednesday: This happened in the 17th century.
The Ming Dynasty had recently ended when it was completed.
Millions of workers built and rebuilt it over centuries.
thursday: This was in the 17th century.
It was built over many dynasties.
friday: The Ming Dynasty oversaw the most famous sections.
It protected against invasions from the north.
saturday: It's visible from space — or so they say.

puzzles.context_card: The Great Wall was built, rebuilt, and maintained over centuries by millions of workers, many of whom died building it. The wall we see today was mostly built during the Ming Dynasty (1368-1644). The total length, including all branches, is over 13,000 miles. Contrary to popular myth, it's not visible from space with the naked eye.

puzzles.headline: The Storming of the Bastille
puzzles.description: Bread prices had skyrocketed and anger at King Louis XVI was boiling over. An armed crowd of Parisians marched on the Bastille — a medieval fortress and symbol of royal tyranny — demanding the weapons stored inside. When negotiations broke down, the crowd attacked. By evening, the fortress had fallen, its governor was killed, and the French Revolution had begun.

monday: This happened during the Enlightenment era.
A medieval fortress in Paris was attacked.
The event led to the end of absolute monarchy in France.
tuesday: The event happened in the summer.
France was ruled by King Louis XVI at the time.
The fortress had become a symbol of royal tyranny.
wednesday: This occurred in the late 18th century.
Rising bread prices fueled the anger.
A major revolution began with this event.
thursday: This was in the 18th century.
The Bastille was a prison in Paris.
friday: It happened in Paris.
A medieval fortress was stormed.
saturday: Bread prices helped start a revolution.

puzzles.context_card: On July 14, 1789, several hundred Parisians stormed the Bastille, a medieval fortress that had become a symbol of royal tyranny. They freed the seven prisoners inside and killed the governor. The event became the defining moment of the French Revolution and is now commemorated annually as Bastille Day, France's national holiday.

puzzles.headline: The Trinity Nuclear Test
puzzles.description: In the predawn darkness of the New Mexico desert, the Manhattan Project reached its climax. A team of scientists and military officials gathered around a steel tower holding the world's first atomic bomb. When the device detonated, the flash was visible for 200 miles and turned sand to glass. J. Robert Oppenheimer later recalled thinking of the Bhagavad Gita: 'Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds.'

monday: This happened in the American Southwest.
The project was called the Manhattan Project.
Robert Oppenheimer was the scientific director.
tuesday: The test was conducted before the end of World War II.
The desert landscape glowed brighter than any light ever seen.
Weeks later, similar devices were used on two Japanese cities.
wednesday: This happened in the 1940s.
The flash was visible 200 miles away.
It was a top-secret military project.
thursday: Robert Oppenheimer led the project.
It was in the 1940s.
friday: The test took place in the New Mexico desert.
The project had been top secret.
saturday: The desert lit up brighter than daylight.

puzzles.context_card: At 5:29 AM on July 16, 1945, J. Robert Oppenheimer watched the Trinity test explode with the words of the Bhagavad Gita in his mind: 'Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds.' The blast was visible 200 miles away. Three weeks later, atomic bombs destroyed Hiroshima and Nagasaki, ending World War II.

puzzles.event_formal: Theft of the Mona Lisa from the Louvre
puzzles.headline: The Mona Lisa Is Stolen from the Louvre
puzzles.description: On a Monday morning, a Louvre worker noticed something alarming: four iron pegs on a wall where the world's most famous painting should have been. Vincenzo Peruggia, a former employee, had hidden in a closet overnight and walked out the next morning with Leonardo da Vinci's masterpiece tucked under his coat. The painting was missing for two years.

monday: The theft made the painting more famous than ever.
The thief was a handyman who worked at the museum.
The painting was missing for two years before being recovered.
tuesday: The artist was an Italian Renaissance master.
The theft was discovered when a worker noticed empty pegs on the wall.
The thief hid in a closet overnight.
wednesday: This happened in the 1910s.
The theft occurred in a famous Paris museum.
The painting was recovered in Florence, Italy.
thursday: This was in the 1910s.
The painting was missing for two years.
friday: The thief was a museum employee.
The painting was found in Italy.
saturday: The thief was a handyman at the Louvre.

puzzles.context_card: Vincenzo Peruggia, a Louvre employee, stole the Mona Lisa by hiding in a broom closet overnight. He simply walked out with it under his coat the next morning. It was missing for two years and was found in Florence in 1913. Paradoxically, the theft transformed the Mona Lisa from a respected painting into a global celebrity.

puzzles.event_formal: Yuri Gagarin becomes first human in space
puzzles.headline: Yuri Gagarin Becomes the First Human in Space
puzzles.description: The 27-year-old Soviet cosmonaut was strapped into Vostok 1 and launched from a cosmodrome in the Kazakh steppe. For 108 minutes he orbited the Earth, becoming the first human to see our planet from space. He described the blue halo of the atmosphere with childlike wonder. Upon re-entry, he ejected from the capsule and parachuted into a farm field, startling a woman and her granddaughter.

monday: This was a major victory in the Space Race.
The cosmonaut was from the Soviet Union.
His flight lasted 108 minutes.
tuesday: His spacecraft made one orbit around Earth.
He said 'Earth is blue' when he saw it from space.
He was only 27 years old at the time.
wednesday: This happened in the 1960s.
The flight launched from a cosmodrome in Kazakhstan.
He parachuted to Earth upon re-entry.
thursday: This was in the 1960s.
He was a Soviet cosmonaut.
friday: The flight lasted 108 minutes.
He orbited the Earth once.
saturday: His famous words were about Earth being blue.

puzzles.context_card: Yuri Gagarin orbited Earth in Vostok 1 on April 12, 1961, completing one orbit in 108 minutes. At 27, he became the first human to see Earth from space. He parachuted from his capsule before it landed — ejection at 7km was standard procedure. A city in Kazakhstan was renamed Baikonur in his honor, and his face became a symbol of Soviet achievement.

puzzles.headline: Google Is Founded
puzzles.description: Stanford PhD students Larry Page and Sergey Brin had a simple but powerful insight: rank web pages by how many other pages link to them, not by counting keywords. They built a prototype search engine in a dorm room, running it on cheap hardware held together by Lego bricks. When they outgrew the university, they moved operations to a friend's garage in Menlo Park.

monday: The company started in a garage — actually, a friend's garage.
The name comes from a misspelling of a mathematical term.
The founders were computer science PhD students.
tuesday: The first investment was $100,000 from an angel investor.
The company began in Menlo Park, California.
Their ranking algorithm was based on links, not keywords.
wednesday: This happened in the late 1990s.
The prototype was built with hardware held together by Lego bricks.
The founders met at Stanford University.
thursday: This was in the late 1990s.
The founders were Stanford students.
friday: The name was a misspelling.
It started in a garage.
saturday: The name was a misspelling of a math term.

puzzles.context_card: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met at Stanford in 1995 and initially didn't get along. Their search algorithm, PageRank, ranked websites by their links rather than keywords. Their first office was a garage rented from a friend. The name 'Google' came from 'googol,' a mathematical term for 1 followed by 100 zeros.

puzzles.event_formal: First recognition of AIDS as a new disease
puzzles.headline: AIDS Is First Identified
puzzles.description: Doctors in Los Angeles and New York began seeing something alarming: otherwise healthy young gay men developing rare cancers and pneumonias that only affected people with severely compromised immune systems. The CDC published a brief report describing five cases of a rare lung infection — a notice that marked the beginning of one of the deadliest pandemics in modern history.

monday: This was first reported in Los Angeles and New York.
Early cases appeared in otherwise healthy young men.
Scientists later identified the virus causing it.
tuesday: The disease was initially called 'GRID' (Gay-Related Immune Deficiency).
A government health bulletin first documented the cases.
By year's end, there were 159 reported cases.
wednesday: This was in the early 1980s.
It would become one of the deadliest pandemics in history.
The virus wouldn't be identified until three years later.
thursday: This was in the early 1980s.
It was first noticed in Los Angeles.
friday: Early cases involved rare pneumonias.
It was initially called GRID.
saturday: It was initially called GRID.

puzzles.context_card: The CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report on June 5, 1981, documented five young gay men in Los Angeles with a rare pneumonia. By the end of 1981, there were 159 reported cases. The disease wouldn't be called AIDS until 1982, and the HIV virus wouldn't be identified until 1984. It would eventually kill 35 million people worldwide.

puzzles.headline: Jeff Bezos Founds Amazon
puzzles.description: A Wall Street analyst quit his lucrative hedge fund job, drove to Seattle, and wrote a business plan in the car. Jeff Bezos had determined that books were the perfect product for online retail — millions of titles, far more than any physical store could stock. Working out of his garage, he built a website called Amazon.com that would grow from selling books to selling everything.

monday: The founder started this in his garage in Seattle.
He originally named it Cadabra.com.
The company went public three years after being founded.
tuesday: Initially, the founder warned investors it would take 5-7 years to profit.
The name was changed because it sounded 'exotic and different.'
The founder left a Wall Street job to start it.
wednesday: This happened in the mid-1990s.
The name was easier to remember than the original choice.
It started as an online bookstore.
thursday: This was in the mid-1990s.
The founder started in his garage.
friday: It was originally named Cadabra.
It started by selling books online.
saturday: It was originally named Cadabra.

puzzles.context_card: Jeff Bezos left his hedge fund job in 1994 to start an online bookstore, originally called Cadabra. He chose the name Amazon because it sounded 'exotic and different,' and the Amazon River was the biggest river he could think of. He raised $1 million from his own parents — the minimum viable investment to start. By 2020, Amazon had a market cap of $1.7 trillion.

puzzles.event_formal: Watson and Crick publish DNA double helix structure
puzzles.headline: Watson and Crick Discover the Structure of DNA
puzzles.description: At the University of Cambridge, James Watson and Francis Crick had been racing to solve the fundamental puzzle of biology: how does life replicate itself? Using X-ray crystallography data from Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51, they built a physical model of a molecule that twisted into an elegant double helix. When they saw the structure, they knew they had found the mechanism of heredity.

monday: This discovery was made in England.
The scientists used X-ray crystallography data from Rosalind Franklin.
The finding was published in the journal Nature.
tuesday: It revolutionized biology and medicine.
The discovery explained how genetic information is passed on.
One scientist was American, the other British.
wednesday: This was in the 1950s.
The molecule has a famous twisted shape.
Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 was critical to the discovery.
thursday: This was in the 1950s.
The discovery was in England.
friday: Rosalind Franklin's data was key.
The discovery was at Cambridge University.
saturday: Rosalind Franklin's data was key.

puzzles.context_card: Watson and Crick published their double helix model in Nature on April 25, 1953. Their famous line: 'It has not escaped our notice that the specific pairing we have postulated immediately suggests a possible copying mechanism for the genetic material.' Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51, which showed the helix structure, was critical — but she died in 1958 and never shared the Nobel Prize.

puzzles.headline: The Chernobyl Disaster
puzzles.description: During a late-night safety test at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in Soviet Ukraine, a series of operator errors and design flaws triggered a catastrophic explosion that ripped open Reactor 4. A plume of radioactive material shot into the atmosphere. The nearby city of Pripyat — population 49,000 — was not evacuated for over 36 hours. Within days, radiation was detected across Europe.

monday: This happened during a safety test at a Ukrainian power plant.
The Soviet government initially tried to cover up the disaster.
The town of Pripyat was evacuated.
tuesday: The explosion sent a plume of radioactive material across Europe.
Firefighters were among the first responders.
The nearby city had a population of nearly 50,000.
wednesday: This occurred in the 1980s.
Ukraine was part of the Soviet Union at the time.
Radiation was detected as far away as Scandinavia.
thursday: This was in the 1980s.
The town of Pripyat was evacuated.
friday: A safety test went wrong.
The disaster was in Ukraine.
saturday: A safety test went wrong.

puzzles.context_card: On April 26, 1986, Reactor 4 at Chernobyl exploded during a low-power test. The fire released radioactive material 9 kilometers into the atmosphere. Soviet authorities evacuated Pripyat (population 49,000) 36 hours later. The final death toll remains disputed — from 31 immediate deaths to estimates of thousands of cancer cases from radiation exposure. The destroyed reactor was finally enclosed in a giant steel sarcophagus in 2016.

puzzles.headline: The First Portable MP3 Player
puzzles.description: Saehan Information Systems, a small electronics company in South Korea, developed the MPMan — a pocket-sized device that could play music stored as compressed digital files. No moving parts, no CD, no tape. Just a small amount of flash memory that could hold about a dozen songs. The device was clunky and expensive, but music was becoming data — and data could be copied and shared endlessly.

monday: This invention came from South Korea.
The technology compressed audio files without major quality loss.
The first model held only about 12 songs.
tuesday: It would take the iPod to make this technology mainstream.
The device had no moving parts.
It used flash memory to store music.
wednesday: This was in the late 1990s.
It was developed by Saehan Information Systems.
The device cost about $250.
thursday: This was in the late 1990s.
It was from South Korea.
friday: It held only about 12 songs.
The iPod later made this mainstream.
saturday: It held only about 12 songs.

puzzles.context_card: The Saehan MP-X, released in 1997, was the first portable MP3 player. It held about 12 songs using flash memory and cost around $250. It had no screen and required a PC to load music. The technology was made mainstream by the iPod in 2001, which Apple co-founder Steve Jobs dismissed as 'just a feature' — meaning a flash-based MP3 player — before reconsidering.

puzzles.headline: The Euro Currency Is Introduced
puzzles.description: Twelve European nations took the most dramatic step in continental integration yet: replacing their individual national currencies with a single shared one. The Deutsche Mark, French Franc, Italian Lira, and nine other currencies were retired in favor of the euro. Centuries of monetary sovereignty were exchanged for the promise of a more unified continent.

monday: This currency replaced multiple national currencies in Europe.
It was first introduced electronically before physical notes appeared.
Germany, France, and Italy were among the original members.
tuesday: Twelve European countries initially adopted it.
Physical notes and coins came a few years after the electronic launch.
The Deutsche Mark and French Franc were among those replaced.
wednesday: This happened at the turn of the millennium.
The currency was designed to unify European markets.
It involved twelve countries giving up their national currencies.
thursday: This was at the turn of the millennium.
Twelve countries first adopted it.
friday: It replaced multiple national currencies.
Germany, France, and Italy were founding members.
saturday: It replaced multiple national currencies.

puzzles.context_card: The Euro was introduced as an accounting currency on January 1, 1999, with physical notes and coins following on January 1, 2002. Twelve countries abandoned their national currencies — including the Deutsche Mark, French Franc, and Italian Lira — for the first time in history. The transition was initially chaotic in some countries, with ATM glitches and confusion at stores.

puzzles.headline: Instagram Launches
puzzles.description: Stanford graduates Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger built a simple mobile app that let people take photos, apply vintage-style filters, and share them in a feed. It launched exclusively on iPhone and reached one million users in just two months — no advertising, no marketing budget. The concept was deceptively simple: make everyone's photos look artistic with one tap.

monday: The app was created by two people who sold it to Facebook for $1 billion.
The name combines 'instant camera' and 'telegram.'
Only iPhone users could use it at first.
tuesday: It popularized square photo filters.
The company had just 13 employees when acquired.
It reached 1 million users in just two months.
wednesday: This was in the early 2010s.
It was acquired by Facebook just 18 months after launch.
The founders met at Stanford University.
thursday: This was in the early 2010s.
It was founded by two people.
friday: It reached 1 million users quickly.
It was acquired for $1 billion.
saturday: It reached 1 million users in just two months.

puzzles.context_card: Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger launched Instagram on October 6, 2010. It reached 1 million users in just two months. Systrom had 12 employees when Facebook acquired it for $1 billion in 2012 — an astonishing valuation for a company with 13 employees and no revenue. By 2018, Instagram had 1 billion users and was Facebook's most valuable asset.

puzzles.headline: The MP3 Format Is Created
puzzles.description: Researchers at the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany developed a method to compress audio files by a factor of 10 or more, stripping out sounds the human ear can't perceive. The resulting files were small enough to transmit over early computer networks. Nobody believed people would want compressed music — the researchers struggled to find anyone interested. They had no idea they'd just created the technology that would upend the entire music industry.

monday: This format compressed audio files by removing sounds humans can't hear.
The researchers worked at the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany.
It made music piracy explode by enabling fast downloads.
tuesday: Napster was built around this format.
A 3.5-minute track could be compressed from 30MB to about 3MB.
The researchers initially couldn't find interested companies.
wednesday: This was in the early 1990s.
It compressed audio roughly 10-12 times without major quality loss.
The format eventually helped destroy physical album sales.
thursday: This was in the early 1990s.
The researchers were from Germany.
friday: Napster used this format.
It compressed audio by removing inaudible frequencies.
saturday: Napster used this format.

puzzles.context_card: The MP3 format was developed by the Fraunhofer Institute in Germany and standardized in 1991. It could compress a 3.5-minute CD track from 30MB to about 3MB. The researchers initially struggled to find companies interested — nobody believed people would want CD-quality music compressed so much. They earned about $1 per licensed player. By 2000, MP3 had destroyed the CD singles market and would eventually help nearly destroy physical album sales.

puzzles.headline: The Wall Street Crash of 1929
puzzles.description: For years, ordinary Americans had been pouring money into the stock market, often buying on margin. Shoeshine boys offered stock tips. Housewives traded shares. Then, on a single October day known as Black Tuesday, panic swept through Wall Street. The Dow fell 12% in one session. Billions of dollars in value evaporated. The Roaring Twenties were over, and the Great Depression had begun.

monday: This happened in October.
Billions of dollars in stock value vanished in days.
The crash ended a decade of reckless speculation.
tuesday: It led to the Great Depression.
Ordinary Americans had been buying stocks on credit.
The Dow fell 12% in a single day.
wednesday: This was in the 1920s.
It was the worst stock market crash in American history at the time.
The Depression that followed saw 25% unemployment.
thursday: This was in the 1920s.
It happened in October.
friday: Billions vanished in days.
It ended the Roaring Twenties.
saturday: Billions vanished in a single day.

puzzles.context_card: On October 29, 1929, investors traded 16 million shares in a panic. The Dow fell 12% in a single day. Over the next few weeks, $30 billion in stock value evaporated — equivalent to about $400 billion today. The crash ended the Roaring Twenties and triggered the Great Depression, which would last a decade and see 25% unemployment in the United States.

puzzles.headline: The First Human Heart Transplant
puzzles.description: In Groote Schuur Hospital in Cape Town, South Africa, Dr. Christiaan Barnard and his surgical team worked through the night to transplant a human heart from a young woman killed in a car accident into 53-year-old Louis Washkansky. When the new heart began beating on its own inside the patient's chest, the operating room fell silent. Washkansky survived the surgery but died 18 days later from pneumonia.

monday: The surgeon was from South Africa.
The operation lasted about 5 hours.
The recipient survived the operation but died 18 days later.
tuesday: The recipient was a 53-year-old with heart disease.
The donor was a woman who died in a car accident.
The surgeon became an overnight celebrity.
wednesday: This was in the 1960s.
The surgeon was Dr. Christiaan Barnard.
The operation took place in Cape Town.
thursday: This was in the 1960s.
The surgeon was from South Africa.
friday: Dr. Christiaan Barnard led it.
It was in Cape Town.
saturday: Dr. Christiaan Barnard led it.

puzzles.context_card: On December 3, 1967, Dr. Christiaan Barnard transplanted a human heart into 53-year-old Louis Washkansky. The operation was successful, but Washkansky died 18 days later from pneumonia — his immune system had been weakened by the anti-rejection drugs. Barnard's face was on magazine covers worldwide overnight. He had gone from obscurity to celebrity in a single operation.

puzzles.event_formal: Salk polio vaccine declared safe and effective
puzzles.headline: The Salk Polio Vaccine Announced
puzzles.description: For years, parents had dreaded the summer, when polio could strike seemingly healthy children, leaving them paralyzed or dead. Jonas Salk spent years developing and testing a vaccine, conducting one of the largest medical trials in history with nearly two million schoolchildren. When the results were announced as safe and effective, church bells rang across America and factory whistles blew.

monday: The vaccine was developed by Jonas Salk.
Parents feared this disease because it paralyzed children.
Church bells rang across America when the news broke.
tuesday: The announcement was made on April 12.
Nearly two million schoolchildren participated in the trial.
Cases dropped by 90% within months.
wednesday: This happened in the 1950s.
The disease especially affected children in summer.
The vaccine trial was one of the largest in history.
thursday: This was in the 1950s.
Jonas Salk developed it.
friday: It was announced on April 12.
Church bells rang across the country.
saturday: It was announced on April 12.

puzzles.context_card: On April 12, 1955, Dr. Thomas Francis announced that Jonas Salk's polio vaccine was 'safe, effective, and potent.' Within hours, churches rang bells and factories blew whistles in celebration. One parent said: 'I have never experienced such a feeling of relief.' Within months, cases of polio in the US dropped by 90%. By 1979, polio was eliminated in the United States.

puzzles.headline: The First ARPANET Email
puzzles.description: Ray Tomlinson, a programmer working on the government-funded ARPANET network, devised a way to send messages between users on different machines. He chose the @ symbol — already on every keyboard — to separate the user name from the machine name, creating an addressing format that billions of people would eventually use. The first message was so mundane he couldn't recall what it said.

monday: The message was sent from Cambridge, Massachusetts.
Ray Tomlinson chose the @ symbol for email addresses.
The exact content of the message has been lost.
tuesday: It was sent over ARPANET, the precursor to the internet.
The sender was testing communication between two machines.
The @ symbol became the defining feature of the format.
wednesday: This was in the early 1970s.
The sender was a computer programmer.
The network connected universities and research labs.
thursday: This was in the early 1970s.
Ray Tomlinson sent it.
friday: The @ symbol was used.
The message traveled over ARPANET.
saturday: The @ symbol was used.

puzzles.context_card: Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email between two computers on ARPANET in 1971. The message itself was mundane — probably 'QWERTYUIOP' or something similar, used as a keyboard test. Tomlinson also chose the @ sign to separate user names from computer names, creating the format we still use today. The invention went from concept to global essential service in under three decades.

puzzles.event_formal: Napoleon Bonaparte crowned Emperor of France
puzzles.headline: Napoleon Crowns Himself Emperor
puzzles.description: In an elaborate ceremony at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France. The Pope had traveled from Rome for the occasion, but in a dramatic gesture of supreme confidence, Napoleon took the crown from the pontiff's hands and placed it on his own head. The message was unmistakable: he owed his power to no one.

monday: The coronation took place at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris.
He famously took the crown from the Pope and placed it on his own head.
The ceremony was painted by Jacques-Louis David.
tuesday: This came after the French Revolution overthrew the monarchy.
The Pope traveled from Rome for the ceremony.
The new emperor had risen from a military career.
wednesday: This was in the early 19th century.
The Pope was present but played a passive role.
His empire would eventually span most of Europe.
thursday: This was in the early 19th century.
The ceremony was at Notre-Dame.
friday: He crowned himself Emperor.
The ceremony was in Paris.
saturday: He crowned himself.

puzzles.context_card: On December 2, 1804, Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France in Notre-Dame. He famously seized the crown from Pope Pius VII and placed it on his own head before crowning his wife Josephine. The painter Jacques-Louis David immortalized the moment in a massive canvas. Napoleon's empire would eventually span most of Europe before his defeat at Waterloo in 1815.

puzzles.headline: YouTube Is Founded
puzzles.description: Three former PayPal employees — Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim — were frustrated by how difficult it was to share video clips online. They built a simple website where anyone could upload and watch videos. The first video ever uploaded was Karim's 18-second clip 'Me at the zoo,' filmed at the San Diego Zoo. Within months, the site was serving millions of videos per day.

monday: The first video was uploaded by a co-founder.
The site was sold to Google 18 months after launch.
The original name consideration was 'Tune In, Hook Up.'
tuesday: The first video was called 'Me at the zoo.'
The co-founders previously worked at PayPal.
Within months, it was serving millions of videos per day.
wednesday: This was in the mid-2000s.
It transformed how media is created and consumed.
Google paid $1.65 billion to acquire it.
thursday: This was in the mid-2000s.
The first video was 'Me at the zoo.'
friday: Google bought it for $1.65 billion.
The co-founders previously worked at a payments company.
saturday: Google bought it for $1.65 billion.

puzzles.context_card: YouTube was founded in February 2005 and launched in November. The first video, 'Me at the zoo,' was uploaded by co-founder Jawed Karim on April 23, 2005, and is still online. Google acquired YouTube in October 2006 for $1.65 billion — at the time, an enormous sum for a company with no revenue and 25 employees. By 2023, YouTube had over 2 billion logged-in monthly users.
